coredata.dc:title: Tunable ternary nanocomposite prepared by electrodeposition for biosensing of centrally acting reversible acetyl cholinesterase inhibitor donepezil hydrochloride in real samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Mohamed, Fardous A.
Khashaba, Pakinaz Y.
Shahin, Reem Y.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: This paper describes the decoration of pencil graphite electrode (PGE) with a thin film of solochrome black T (SBT) and N-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs)/ cobalt nanoparticles (CoNPs) to amplify signal of the measurement. This nanocomposite was chosen to enhance surface activity and electron transfer toward donepezil HCl (DZ HCl) oxidation. The physical properties of nanocomposite were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), electron impedance spectroscopy (ESI) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy. Cyclic voltammetry was utilized to quantify DZ HCl at the modified PGE in Britton-Robinson buffer (pH = 6.5). Under optimized experimental conditions, peak current increased with the increase in the concentration of DZ HCl and was linear between the range of 1.5 nM–400 μM with a relatively low detection limit of 0.0005 μM. Due to high sensitivity, simplicity and cost-effectiveness of the designated sensor; it was used as a simple method for detecting DZ HCl in rabbit plasma and during its pharmacokinetic study.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Donepezil HCl
Poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs
Cyclic voltammetry
Pharmacokinetic study

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs)
In vivo study: pharmacokinetic study of DZ HCl in rabbit plasma
UV and fluorescence spectra of N-CNDs
Factors affecting synthesis of the poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE
Cyclic voltammetry of the modified PGEs
Electrochemical oxidation of DZ HCl at poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/ CoNPs/PGE and parameters affecting its oxidation
Electrochemical behavior of DZ HCl at the poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/ CoNPs/PGE
Parameters affecting oxidation of DZ HCl at poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE
Effect of supporting electrolytes and pH
Validation of the proposed method for DZ HCl detection
Application to pharmaceutical tablets and rabbit plasma

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The chemical formulas of precursor materials used for voltammetric analysis of DZ HCl.
SEM images of (A) Bare PGE, (B) poly (SBT)/PGE, (C) poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/PGE (C) and (D) poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/PGE/CoNPs.

(A) The UV-VIS and (B) fluorescence spectra of aqueous solution of N-CNDs where insets of (B) are pictures of N-CNDs under daylight and UV lamp. (C) FTIR spectra of: (a) poly (SBT), (b) N-CNDs and (c) poly (SBT)/N-CNDs. (D) and (E) are EIS and CV of (a) PGE, (b) poly (SBT)/PGE, (c) poly (SBT)/N-CNDs /PGE and poly(SBT)/N-CNDs/ CoNPs/PGE using 2.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1 M KCl, respectively. (F) EDX of N-CNDs.

(A) The CV of (1) Bare PGE, (2) poly (SBT)/PGE, (3) poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/PGE and (4) poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE in B.R. buffer (pH 6.5) at scan rat of 100 mV s−1 and (B) Linear relationship between current and scan rate in CV curves.

The CV of poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE (a and e), bare PGE (b), poly (SBT)/PGE (c) and poly (SBT)/N-CNDs (d) in B.R. buffer (pH 6.5) without (a) or with (b–e) 100 μM DZ HCl. Initial accumulation time and scan rate were 180 s and 200 mV s−1.

(a) The effect of different pH values on oxidation of 100 μM DZ HCl at optimized conditions: A. pH = 4.0, B. pH = 5.5, C. pH = 6.5, D. pH = 7, (b) Relationships between pH vs. current and (c) pH vs. potential. Initial accumulation time and scan rate were 180 s and 200 mV s−1.
(A) Effect of scan rate on oxidation of 100 μM DZ HCl, (B) Plot relationship between scan rate and current, (C) Relationship between ν ½ and log Ipa and (D) Relationship between ν ½ and Epa.

(a) The CV of DZ HCl at the poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE after addition of different concentrations (10–400 μM) in B.R. buffer (pH 6.5) while (b) is a calibration curve. Initial accumulation time and scan rate were 180 s and 200 mV s−1.
Analytical quantitative parameters for oxidation of DZ HCl at poly(SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE.
The selectivity of the proposed method for analysis of 100 μg of DZ HCl in its tablets using poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE.
Comparison between the proposed CV methods with other reported methods.

head.ce:title: Tunable ternary nanocomposite prepared by electrodeposition for biosensing of centrally acting reversible acetyl cholinesterase inhibitor donepezil hydrochloride in real samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Deraya University, El-Minya, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Research Center, Assiut University, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper describes the decoration of pencil graphite electrode (PGE) with a thin film of solochrome black T (SBT) and N-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs)/ cobalt nanoparticles (CoNPs) to amplify signal of the measurement. This nanocomposite was chosen to enhance surface activity and electron transfer toward donepezil HCl (DZ HCl) oxidation. The physical properties of nanocomposite were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), electron impedance spectroscopy (ESI) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy. Cyclic voltammetry was utilized to quantify DZ HCl at the modified PGE in Britton-Robinson buffer (pH = 6.5). Under optimized experimental conditions, peak current increased with the increase in the concentration of DZ HCl and was linear between the range of 1.5 nM–400 μM with a relatively low detection limit of 0.0005 μM. Due to high sensitivity, simplicity and cost-effectiveness of the designated sensor; it was used as a simple method for detecting DZ HCl in rabbit plasma and during its pharmacokinetic study.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a neurodegenerative disease (loss of structure and function of neurons) associated with age and is the most common adult form of dementia [1]. As a result of increasing advances in medical sciences, people are born to live longer and thus are more susceptible to AD. For many years, the disease had no treatment [2] and the drugs given might improve only the symptoms associated with AD. In the last twenty years, the situation has dramatically changed by the introduction of cholinesterase inhibitors (ChEIs) which inhibit the acetylcholine degradation facilitating the cholinergic neurotransmission [3]. The ChEIs includes four drugs: tacrine, rivastigmine, donepezil and galantamine. Tacrine was the first line of symptomatic treatment but its hepatotoxicity and poor tolerability limits its use. Nowadays, DZ is the first drug of choice in symptomatic treatment of mild to moderate AD [4], (Fig. 1S).

It has the longest absorption time of all ChEIs, reaching a peak plasma time in 3–5 h [5]. Following the initiation of daily therapy, it accumulates four or seven folds until a steady state concentration is reached within 15–21 days [2]. Most common side effects include sleep disturbances, headache and GIT upset and all these symptoms were well tolerated and disappeared during continued treatment with DZ HCl without need for therapy [6].

Many methods were proposed for DZ HCl analysis including spectrophotometric methods [7,8], HPLC methods [9,10], spectrodensitometric method [11] and electrochemical methods [12–14]. Electrochemical methods have recently drawn so much attention due to their simplicity, low analysis time / cost and absence of analyte extraction if compared to others [15,16].

The use of bare electrode in voltammetric analysis has many limitations such as electrode fouling, slow electron transfer, lack of reproducibility, low sensitivity and selectivity and high over potential [17,18]. Therefore, different methods and materials have recently been used for the modification of the electrodes [19–21].

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been widely used due to their low toxicity, water compatibility, excellent optical and chemical stability and biocompatibility [22–24], but it was found that their band gab and electron density need to be well toned (controlled) to improve their chemical and optical properties [25]. The introduction of heteroatom is an ideal solution for modulating the intrinsic properties of CNDs [26,27]. Nitrogen is an ideal dopant for modulating the optical and chemical properties of CDs because its molecular size is comparable to that of carbon. It also has five valence electrons to combine with the carbon atom, thus more electrons are available for interaction with the carbon dots resulting in a change in its optical and electronic transport properties [28–31]. Heteroatom doped CDs are mainly used as fluorescent probe in sensing, catalytic and biological applications [32–35].

Due to good stability, homogeneity and good adherence; polymers have played important roles in electrode modification [36,37]. Moreover, polymeric films enhance the oxidation and prevent undesirable reactions. Their presence can be maintained and optimized by adjusting electrochemical parameters [38–40]. From many works, we can deduce that electrodes modified with redox dyes show excellent stability, reproducibility and homogeneity [41–43]. These were considered as good electron donors [44,45] and able to undergo facile electro-polymerization process [46,47]. One of these is polymerized solochrome black T [poly (SBT)] that has great amounts of negatively charged sulfite groups on the electrode surface and has a potential for incorporation of functionalities against measured analytes [48]. Also, formation of selective coatings can discriminate between analytes through hydrophobic and hydrophilic difference, electrostatic interaction or ion-exchange abilities. Recently, poly (SBT) modified electrodes were used for voltammetric analysis of pharmaceuticals, metals and biologically active compounds such as uric acid, dopamine and ascorbic acid [49], epinephrine [50] and simultaneous analysis of anti-ulcer and D2-antagonist agents [51].

In this paper, we describe a highly sensitive and reproducible method for analysis of DZ HCl in rabbit plasma and during its pharmacokinetic study using PGE modified with N-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs), poly (SBT)/cobalt nanoparticles (CoNPs). To our knowledge, this is the first paper that describes polymerization of SBT/N-CNDs in presence of CoNPs as a ternary nanocomposite for the bioanalysis of DZ HCl. The synthesized nanocomposite has shown higher sensitivity, selectivity, reproducibility and stability towards DZ HCl analysis if compared with other electroanalytical methods. Moreover, the method was used to monitor DZ HCl during its pharmacokinetic study in rabbit plasma.
Donepezil HCl was obtained as a gift from NODCAR (National Organization of Drug Control and Research), El-Giza, Egypt. Aricept® tablets (Pfizer, Cairo, Egypt) are labeled to contain10 mg DZ.

Solochrome black T, sodium hydroxide, glacial acetic acid, phosphoric acid, boric acid, sodium acetate, cobalt nitrate, citric acid and ethylene diamine, starch, glucose, sucrose, gum acacia, ascorbic acid and oxalic acid were purchased from El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co., Egypt. Dopamine, methionine and uric acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany. Double distilled water was used throughout this work.

A Princeton VersaSTAT MC (VersaSTAT 3, Model RE-1, Princeton Applied Research, AMETEK, USA) connected to a three-electrode cell was used for the electrochemical measurements. In all measurements, the reference electrode was Ag/ AgCl (3 M KCl) while the auxiliary electrode was a platinum wire and bare or modified PGE was used as working electrode. A Pentel pencil, Model P205 (Japan), was used as a holder for the pencil lead. Electrical contact with the lead was achieved by soldering a metallic wire to the metallic part that holds the lead in place inside the pencil. Measurements were performed in a 10-mL glass cell containing 6 ml of Britton Robinson (B.R.) buffer solution (pH = 6.5). Stirring was achieved with a magnetic stirring bar. The pH values of solutions were measured using Hanna pH meter (Hanna Instruments Brazil, Sao˜ Paulo, Brazil) with a combined electrode. The solutions were sonicated using Bransonic ultrasonic cleaner, Branson UL Transonics Corporation, Eagle Road, Danbury, CT 06813, USA. Surface morphology studies of the modified electrode were carried out using scanning electron microscope (SEM), JEOL JSM-5400 LV instrument (Oxford, USA). A Nicolet 6700 FTIR advanced Gold Spectrometer, supported with OMNIC 8 software (Thermo Electron Scientific Instruments Corp., WI USA) for data processing. Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometer (EDX) (OXFORD INA) was used to analyze N-CNDs elements.

An accurately weighed amount of DZ HCl equivalent to 1.0 mM was transferred into 100 mL calibrated volumetric flask containing 50 mL double distilled water, shaken well and then completed to volume with double distilled water. The working standard solutions were prepared by further dilution of the suitable aliquots of the stock solution with B.R. buffer (pH = 6.5) in the range of (10–400 μM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs)
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of 1.05 g citric acid and 5 mL ethylene diamine were heated in autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h. The obtained solution was filtered using medium speed filter paper to remove undissolved black parts. The prepared solution was poured into a beaker containing 0.3 N NaOH and the pH value was adjusted to 7. The solution was then diluted to 100 mL double distilled water and placed in a refrigerator until its use.

New polished surface of PGE was immersed in a cell containing equal volumes of 0.5 mM SBT (dissolved in 0.1 N NaOH) and 1 mg/mL N-CNDs (aqueous solution) and then electro-polymerization process was conducted at the following optimal conditions: accumulation potential range= −1.8 to 1.8 V, scan rate = 120 mVs−1, accumulation time = 10 s and number of polymerization cycles = 8 cycles. The constructed electrode was dried in air for 2 min and then immersed in a solution containing 0.04 mM cobalt nitrate solution at accumulation potential= −0.75 V for 50 s as accumulation time.
The chemical formulas of all the precursor materials used for voltammetric determination of DZ HCl were presented in Fig. 1 .

An aliquot of standard solution containing DZ HCl was transferred into the electrochemical cell containing modified PGE and B.R. buffer (pH = 6.5) and measured using cyclic voltammetry under optimized conditions: accumulation potential=−0.1, accumulation time = 180 s and scan rate = 200 mV s−1.

Ten tablets were accurately weighed and finely powdered. An amount of powdered tablets equivalent to 1.0 mM of DZ was transferred into 100 mL volumetric flask and dissolved in 50 mL double distilled water (solubility of DZ HCl in water, 2.93 mg/L at 25 °C). The mixture was sonicated for 30 min, filtered in 100 mL volumetric flask. The residue was washed three times with double distilled water and then filtrate was diluted to volume with the same solvent. Working standard solutions were prepared from the stock standard solution by serial dilution with B.R. buffer (pH = 6.5).

Drug free plasma was obtained from six healthy rabbits and stored at −20ºC to the next day after collection. Before analysis, plasma was treated with 0.5 mL acetonitrile to precipitate insoluble proteins. Then, one milliliter of plasma clear solution was quantitatively transferred into voltammetric cell containing 6.0 mL B.R buffer pH = 6.5 (% of plasma = 17%) and DZ HCl. Each measurement was carried using new polished and modified PGE. The procedure was then followed as described in the general procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo study: pharmacokinetic study of DZ HCl in rabbit plasma
ce:section.ce:para: Six healthy male rabbits, weighing 1.8–2 K g, were used in this study. Tablets were ground and the required animal dose (0.13 mg/Kg) was weighed and suspended in distilled water. The dispersion was then administered to rabbits orally. Following oral administration, blood samples were drawn from retro-orbital plexus of rabbits using fine capillary at predosing, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 and 24 h. Samples were collected in screw capped sterile heparinized centrifuge tubes. Plasma was separated from each sample by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 15 min. Labeled plasma samples were stored in polypropylene tubes at −20 °C until being analyzed. The collected plasma samples were analyzed as described in the general procedure. The experimental protocol was approved by the Institutional Animal Ethics Committee, Assiut University for the use of experimental animals and all animal studies were carried out according to the Guide for Care and Use of Laboratory Animal.

The synthesis of modified PGE was studied and confirmed using SEM technique. Fig. 2 shows a comparison of SEM images of bare PGE, poly (SBT)/PGE, poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/PGE and poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE. Fig. 2A show the bare PGE with the absence of deposited nanomaterials while the presence of uniform layer on PGE in Fig. 2B proves the deposition of SBT on the electrode. The formation of coarse and homogenous particles with average size of 120 nm in Fig. 2C confirms the formation of N-CNDs and SBT on the surface of SBT. Moreover, the presence of dispersed nanoparticles in Fig. 2D with average size of 50 nm confirms the deposition of CoNPs in the formed nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV and fluorescence spectra of N-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the synthesis of N-CNDs, UV and fluorescence spectra of aqueous N-CNDs were recorded. From Fig. 3 A shows the presence of two broad bands at 240 and 360 nm which could be attributed to π-π* of CC and n-π*of CO originally present in N-CNDs. The fluorescence spectra of N-CNDs, it was obvious that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were 290 and 390 nm, respectively (Fig. 3B). For further confirmation, the dark yellow color of N-CNDs was measured under ambient conditions and UV lamp (Inset of Fig. 3B).

The FTIR spectra of 3.0 mg poly (SBT), N-CNDs and poly (SBT)/N-CNDs were represented in Fig. 3C. These characteristic bands were used as a confirmation for the synthesis of nanomaterials. The characteristic δ(N = N), ν (CC), ν(C–H) and ν (OH, NH2 or COOH) that appear at 1094, 1629, 2924 and 3450 cm−1 are due to polymerized SBT (Fig. 3Ca). While the bands that feature N- doped carbon nanodots: 3422, 2915, 1660, 1340 and 1115 cm−1 are due to ν (OH, NH2 or COOH), ν(CH), ν (CC or CO), ν (CN of amide) and ν (CN of amine) as shown in Fig. 3Cb. The combination FTIR between poly (SBT) and N-CNDs with their characteristic bands are represented in Fig. 3Cc.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy is a powerful technique that describes the charge transfer resistance between electrode/ solution interface. So, different electrodes were immersed in 2.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− dissolved in 0.1 M KCl as a supporting electrolyte. Fig. 3D shows that the increase in impedance in the order of PGE, poly(SBT)/PGE, poly(SBT)/N-CNDs/PGE while poly(SBT)/N-CNDs/ CoNPs/PGE has shown the least charge transfer resistance and this may be due to higher electron transfer introduced by nanomaterials.

Cyclic voltammetry was used to show the effect of electrodeposited nanoparticles on the electro-oxidation of 2.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− dissolved in 0.1 M KCl. From Fig. 3E, it is seen that the anodic and cathodic oxidation of redox probe increases with deposition of different nanoparticles. The charges (q) associated with the deposition of different layers were calculated by integrating the peak area under absence of Faradic current and found to be 48.8, 55.7, 63.7 and 74.5 mC cm2− for bare PGE, poly(SBT)/PGE, poly(SBT)/N-CNDs/PGE while poly(SBT)/N-CNDs/ CoNPs/PGE, respectively.

To confirm the good synthesis of N-CNDs and inclusion of nitrogen within the core of CNDs nanocomposite, EDX is a useful tool for analysis of the formed elements. Fig. 3 F shows definite identifiable peaks of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen that proved the formation of N-CNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting synthesis of the poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE
ce:section.ce:para: See supporting materials

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammetry of the modified PGEs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 A shows the CV curves of unmodified and modified PGE in B.R. buffer (pH 6.5) in potential range of −0.2 to 1.0 V. The CV of PGE modified with poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs shows the largest charging current than the other electrodes. The electrode responses to potential change follow the double layer capacity behavior, and all PGE can be used as electrochemical capacitors. The linear relations are I0.1 = 0.51ν-4.45, I0.1 = 0.64ν-3.34, I0.1 = 0.96ν-3.93 and I0.1 = 1.20 ν-3.32 for bare PGE, poly (SBT)/PGE, poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/PGE and poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE, respectively. The slope for the modified poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE is larger than that of the others. This larger slope indicates that the response of poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE to potential change is large than that of others, and with the increase of scan rate the current must further increase faster than that of the bare PGE, poly (SBT)/PGE and poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/PGE (Fig. 4B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical oxidation of DZ HCl at poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/ CoNPs/PGE and parameters affecting its oxidation
Electrochemical behavior of DZ HCl at the poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/ CoNPs/PGE
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical behavior of 100 μM of DZ HCl was studied by CV in potential range of −0.1 to 1.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl, 3 M KCl) using B.R. buffer (pH = 6.5). It is obvious that the presence of one anodic peak for DZ HCl at 0.76 V and no cathodic peak in reversed scan that confirms the process of oxidation is chemically irreversible (Fig. 5 ). Moreover, the decoration of the PGE with different modifiers has resulted in increased oxidation current of DZ HCl in the order of PGE, poly (SBT)/PGE, poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/PGE, and finally poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE. The results ensured that the modification of PGE with three modifiers resulted in enhancement of surface area for oxidation and increased charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameters affecting oxidation of DZ HCl at poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE

ce:section.ce:para: Accumulation potential and time give more information about availability of the analyte to accumulate at the electrode surface. So, different potentials were studied in the range of −0.7 to 0.4 V where maximum oxidation current was obtained at −0.1 V (Figure 7SA). Moreover, the effect of accumulation times was investigated in the range of 20–270 s where the maximum DZ HCl capacitive current was obtained at an accumulation time of 180 s (Figure 7SB).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of supporting electrolytes and pH
ce:section.ce:para:
Several buffers were tested to show their effects on oxidation behavior of DZ HCl at poly (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE such as acetate, borate, phosphate and B.R. buffers (pH = 6.5). The maximum oxidation current was attained with Britton-Robison buffer (pH = 6.5) as shown in Fig. 8S. Moreover, different pH values of B.R. buffer were used to investigate the effect of pH on the DZ HCl behavior at (SBT)/N-CNDs/CoNPs/PGE. It was found that the oxidation peak of DZ HCl appeared in pH range of 4.0–7.0 while the maximum oxidation occurred at pH = 6.5 (Fig. 6 ). In addition, the oxidation potential (Epa) of the drug was shifted to lesser values upon increasing pH of the medium. These shifts may be due to participation of proton during the electrode reaction. The linear equation is as follows:
(1) Epa = 0.039pH + 1.01

Cyclic voltammetry of DZ HCl oxidation at the modified electrode in B.R. buffer (pH = 6.5) was studied under various scan rates. As shown in Fig. 7 A, it was obvious that increasing scan rate in the range 20–200 mV s−1 is followed by increasing the oxidation current of the drug and shifting the potential to more positive values. A linear plot was obtained between scan rate and anodic current that proved that the oxidation mechanism was adsorption controlled (Fig. 7B). The obtained results were confirmed by plotting logarithm scan rate against anodic current (Ipa). The plot has a slope of 1.31 which is closer to the theoretical value (1.0) for adsorption controlled mechanism [52] (Fig. 7C). The regression equation between log anodic current and log scan rate ( ν ) at the modified electrode is:
(2) log Ipa = 1.31 log ν + 3.41 (R2 = 0.9977)

Moreover, plot relationship between log scan rate and log Epa is represented in Fig. 7D and obeys the following equation:
(3) log Epa = 0.048 log ν + 0.84

For irreversible and diffusion-controlled oxidation process, the number of transferred electrons (n) can be derived from the following equation [53]:
(4)
E =
E
o '
+
R T
α n F
1 n
R T
k
e t
α n F
−
R T
α n F
1 n
v
Where α: transfer coefficient, ν: scan rate, Eº/: formal potential, n: number of electrons transferred, T: temperature in Kelvin, F: faradaic constant and Ket: is the standard rate constant of the reaction in s−1. According to the Bard and Faulkner's publication [54], the transfer coefficient α is generally between 0.3 and 0.7, and the transferred electron number is adapted to be positive integer, so α should be 0.58 while n equals 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the proposed method for DZ HCl detection
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was validated according to International Conference on Harmonization (ICH) guidelines [55] in the term of limits of detection (LOD) and quantitation (LOQ), accuracy, precision, selectivity and robustness.

Cyclic voltammetry was used to test the effect of increasing concentrations on the anodic oxidation of DZ HCl. From Fig. 8 , it was seen that the increase in concentration of DZ HCl resulted in increasing the stripping currents of the drug in the range of 2–400 μM. The LOD and LOQ were calculated using the following formula:
(5) LOQ = 10 SDb/S while LOD = 3.3 SDb/S
Where SDb is standard deviation of intercept while S is slope of calibration curve
Important parameters of calibration curve were summarized in Table 1 .

Accuracy of the method was confirmed by standard addition technique. Where known amount of pure standard was added to tablet sample and then calculating the recovery percentages. The results summarized in Table 1S indicate that the proposed method exhibits good accuracy and no interference from excipients.

Repeatability (intraday precision) and reproducibility (interday precision) were measured by calculating % relative standard deviation (%RSD). The precision of the proposed method is confirmed by the resulted small values of % RSD calculated in Table 2S.

The effect of common interfering substances was investigated using CV technique by addition of high concentrations of common excipients, anions, cations and biologically active substances. Tolerance ratios were calculated and summarized in Table 2 where the results confirmed that addition of large amount of interfering substances does not significantly affect the original peak current of the drug. The changes recorded in the oxidation current do not exceed 5% that means that the proposed method can determine DZ in high concentration of interfering substances.

The ability of the method to resist small change in the analytical procedures was evaluated by slight change in pH, deposition time, concentrations of SBT, cobalt and N-CNDs and scan rate. The results obtained in Table 3S confirm the ability of method to resist small changes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to pharmaceutical tablets and rabbit plasma

ce:section.ce:para: The method was applied for determination of the cited drug in its pharmaceutical tablets and the results were compared with reported method [13]. The recoveries obtained were 101.6 ± 2.02 and 98.8 ± 1.98 for proposed and reported methods, respectively. The F- and t- values obtained were 4.01 and 1.89 that are lower than the theoretical values of 6.39 and 2.77, respectively, at confidence level of 95% (n = 5).
The linearity of the proposed method was 15–350 μM with LOD and LOQ of 0.001 and 0.003 μM, respectively.

The accuracy of the proposed method was determined by calculating the recovery percentages of at three concentration levels. The results of 98.9–104.6% (% RSD ranged from 2.55 to 3.23 %) revealed good accuracy of the proposed method.

Intraday precision was calculated at three concentration levels and the recoveries percentages obtained were in the range of 97.7–102.3% (% RSD ranged from 1.98 to 2.98 %). Moreover, interday precision was measured with recoveries percentages of 99.3–101.4% (% RSD ranged from 2.05 to 3.02%).

The CV was used for the first time to determine the pharmacokinetic parameters of DZ HCl in rabbits plasma due to the high sensitivity of the proposed method, which is expressed by low LOD and LOQ values following the oral administration of Aricept® tablets containing DZ HCl at a dose of 0.13 mg/Kg (n = 6) (Figure 9S). Pharmacokinetic parameters shown in Table 4S were calculated by a non-compartmental statistics model using moment analysis. The first-order terminal elimination rate constant (Kel) was estimated by linear regression from the points describing the elimination phase on a log-linear plot. The maximum observed plasma concentration (Cmax) and the time taken to achieve this maximum level (Tmax) were obtained directly from the curves. The areas under the curve for DZ HCl plasma concentration versus time for 0–12 h (AUC0-12 h) were calculated by applying the linear trapezoidal method. The extrapolation of this area to infinity (AUC0-inf) was done by adding the value C12/Kel to the calculated AUC0-12 where C12 is the DZ HCl plasma concentration at 12 h and Kel is the first-order terminal elimination rate constant.

The stability of the modified electrode was investigated after storage of the prepared electrode for several weeks in refrigerator. From Figure 10 S, it was found the electrode retains its activity for three weeks after that the functionality of the electrode began to diminish.

The electrochemical oxidation of DZ HCl was previously studied at glassy carbon electrode [14] and recently at gold electrode [15] in which oxidation occurred at pH 3 and two anodic reactions were observed. The first main anodic reaction at potential of 1.15 V could be attributed to the oxidation of the nitrogen atom on the piperidinyl moiety in DZ HCl. While the second at potential of 1.4 V was assigned to the oxidation of alkoxybenzene. Meanwhile, It was suggested that oxidation of alkane to alcohol at Pt electrode [18] proceeds to the formation of secondary carbonium ion in the presence of an acid, that further reacts with solvent, water, to form an alcohol. Due to similarity of condition i.e. the presence of acid and water, identification of the major bulk electrolyte after the electrochemical reactions was performed by high resolution mass spectrometry and 1D and 2D NMR and DZ hydroxy derivative was identified as the major electrochemical oxidation product from DZ. This compound has been reported as intermediate in various processes for the synthesis of DZ [13,14] and has been investigated for acetylcholine esterase inhibitory activity [15]. In our study, optimum anodic reaction being carried out at potential of 0.8 v and at higher pH value of 6.5, the possible oxidation mechanism of DZ HCl at the modified PGE is more likely to be due to oxidation of nitrogen atom at the piperidinyl ring and this may be attributed to electron rich nitrogen that facilitates oxidation at this site to form N-oxide derivative [14], Scheme 1S.

The proposed method was compared to other electrochemical methods for determination of DZ HCl (Table 3 ), it is clear that the proposed method offers greater sensitivity and hence good applicability if compared to other methods.

A modified PGE was developed for the determination of DZ HCl. The combined advantages of poly (SBT), N-CNDs and CoNPs enhanced conductivity and surface area improving the overall performance of the electrode. Under optimum conditions, the method offered good sensitivity, stability, reproducibility, and selectivity. It was successfully applied for the analysis of DZ HCl in pharmaceutical dosage form and rabbit plasma during pharmacokinetic study with good recoveries. Application of the procedure in rabbit plasma shows the lack of interfering oxidation compounds or extra peaks in the potential range used for the determination of DZ HCl. Therefore, no sample pretreatment was required, only dilution of the sample with the selected supporting electrolyte. Compared to HPLC and spectrophotometric methods, the proposed method requires no time consuming steps such as extraction or evaporation and is considered a good alternative to other analytical approaches for determination of DZ HCl in plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2019.01.033.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
